content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 5, 2016

Nick Clair
Legislative Analyst
California Special Districts Association
11121 Street, Suite 200
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-115

Dear Mr. Clair:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be
taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter.
This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request letter.

QUESTION

Will raffle prizes won by public officials in three separate random drawings at the CSDA
Annual Conference attended by public officials and other individuals not regulated by the Act be
considered gifts to those public officials?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The raffle prizes will be received in a bona fide competition related to the official
status of the public officials, and the prize donations will constitute a "gift" to those officials within
the meaning of the Act.

FACTS

You are seeking advice on behalf of the California Special Districts Association ("CSDA")
and would like to know whether raffle prizes awarded to public officials at the CSDA Annual
Conference are "gifts" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82028, or whether such
prizes qualify for the exception defined in Regulation 18942(a)(14). We take the following facts
verbatim from your letter:

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-115
Page No. 2

CSDA is a 501(c)(6) not-for-profit association that was formed to
promote good governance and improved core local serves through
professional development, advocacy, and other services for all types of
independent special districts. CSDA has three types of membership:
regular members, associate members, and business affiliates. Regular
membership is open only to independent special districts, as defined by
Government Code Section 56044, and other public agencies whose
governing body is composed of representatives of two or more public
agencies except joint powers authorities, dependent special districts, and
county and city agencies. Associate membership is open to public
agencies which do not meet the eligibility requirements for regular
membership. Business affiliates are private sector companies that offer
professional services and products to the special district community. All
membership classes require the payment of dues.

CSDA currently has 1,104 total members. Of those, 833 are regular
members, 137 are associate members, and 115 are business affiliates.

CSDA holds an annual conference with the primary purposes to provide
educational opportunities for special district board members and
employees and to provide a venue for networking among special district
board members, employees, and industry experts. This event is open to
all CSDA members, non-members, and the general public. The
conference occurs over the course of three days.

The 2015 CSDA Annual Conference is representative of these
conferences generally, and is representative of expectations for the
coming 2016 CSDA Annual Conference.

The price for regular Full Conference attendance for CSDA Members
(including regular members, associate members, and business affiliates)
was $600 for the 2015 CSDA Annual Conference. The price for regular
Full Conference attendance for Non-members was $800. The price for
regular Full Conference attendance for Guests (does not include
employees or officers of government entities or business affiliates) was
$300. The price for a regular One-Day attendance for CSDA Members
was $275 per day. The price for a regular One-Day attendance for Non-
members was $435 per day. Discounted pricing was available for early
registration.

Total attendance at the 2015 CSDA Conference was 768. The majority
of these attendees were employees or board members of special districts
or other local governments. 28 attendees were given free attendance and
the majority, if not all, of these were employees or representatives of
other local government associations and not public officials. Guests
accounted for 7 full conference attendees (most of these were likely
guests of public officials), and 49 single day guests. Approximately 133

File No. A-16-115
Page No. 3

of the attendees were exhibitors at the conference and most, if not all,
were business affiliate members and not public officials.

Three types of raffles occurred at the 2015 CSDA Annual Conference
and it is anticipated that the same types of raffles will occur at the
coming 2016 Annual Conference. This first type involves the purchase
of raffle tickets, at a cost of a few dollars per ticket. This raffle is open to
all CSDA Conference Attendees, including public officials, their guests,
members of the public, and business affiliates. Each ticket has a unique
number. The ticket purchaser keeps one half of the ticket and the other
half is placed with the others in the raffle. No names or identifying
information is recorded on the ticket or anywhere else. The raffle is
conducted during exhibit hall hours - times in which attendees are
encourage to visit with business affiliates who have booths advertising
their products and services. The raffle is conducted by a random drawing
of raffle tickets during the exhibit hall hours and in full view of all of
those in attendance. The number on the winning raffle ticket is
announced via a public address system to the whole exhibit hall. Those
who purchased tickets must be present in the exhibit hall in order to
claim the prize and, if the holder of the winning raffle ticket number is
not present or does not come forward to claim the prize, another ticket is
randomly drawn for that same prize. Prizes are generally consumer
goods of under $50 dollars in value. We have no way of knowing the
proportion of public officials, guests, or business affiliates who purchase
tickets or are present during the raffle. It is likely that raffle participant
types roughly correspond with the types of conference attendees.

The second type of raffle is an "exhibitor drawing" which is also
conducted in the exhibitor hall during exhibit hall hours. All conference
attendees can obtain an "exhibitor card" which lists each of the
exhibitors in the hall. Attendees can present this card when visiting each
exhibitor booth and the exhibitor will place a mark on the card indicating
that the attendee has visited the corresponding booth. After receiving a
mark from each exhibitor, the attendees can deposit their completed
exhibitor card in a box from which one card is randomly selected. This
raffle is conducted at the same time and in the same manner as the first
described raffle. Because all attendees may obtain an exhibitor card and
no records are kept as to who actually completes a card and participates
in the raffle, we are unable to determine the proportion of public
officials, guests, or business affiliates who submit completed cards and
participate in the raffle.

The third type of raffle is a raffle conducted by the Special District
Leadership Foundation ("SDLF"). SDLF is a not-for-profit 501(c)(3)
organization which provides programs and certifications to special
districts and their staffs. This raffle is conducted during a reception
hosted by SDLF on the second night of the Annual Conference. The

File No. A-16-115
Page No. 4

reception and raffle are open to all conference attendees. Reception
attendees can purchase raffle tickets from the SDLF and the raffle is
conducted near the end of the reception. The raffle is conducted in a
manner similar to the first of the above described raffles and we
similarly have no information as to which types of attendees purchase
tickets and participate in the raffle.

ANALYSIS

The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as:

Any payment² that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the
rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.

In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,³ the Act regulates the receipt
of gifts by public officials in the following ways:

First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The
current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.)
This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals designated in
Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee designated in his
or her agency's conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, if the employee
would be required to disclose the receipt of income or gifts from the source of the gift on his or her
statement of economic interest. (Section 89503.)

Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any gifts that
aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on
the officials' statements of economic interests. Reporting requirements apply to all officials listed in
Section 87200 (Section 87202), all candidates for an office specified in Section 87200 (Section
87201), and employees designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code as specified in the code
(Section 87302(b)).

Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift
or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the
official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100, 87103(e),
Regulation 18700.)

² Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any "rendering of ... services or anything else of value, whether
tangible or intangible."

³ A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental
agency." (Section 82048.)

File No. A-16-115
Page No. 5

However, there are also exceptions to the definition of a gift. One such exception occurs
when a prize or award is "received in a manner not related to the official's status in a bona fide
contest, competition, or game of chance. A prize or award that is not reported as a gift shall
be reported as income . . . ."4 (Regulation 18942(a)(14).)

As long as the raffles will be conducted in such a way that the winner of the prize is selected
by random chance, which it appears all three in this matter will be, the raffles would be considered
bona fide competitions. (Rose Advice Letter, No. A-03-151; Gilliland Advice Letter, No. A-01-225;
Nemeroff Advice Letter, No. A-99-148; Allen Advice Letter, No. A-95-188.)

The more problematic concern in your question is whether, if a public official wins, the
competitions are considered related to his or her status as a public official. One of the main factors
in making this determination is the pool of contestants in the competition. For example, in the
Pavlovich Advice Letter, No. A-94-391, the League of California Cities held a membership dinner
for approximately 100 people consisting of elected city officials and city staff, and their spouses or
guests. Each attendee received a raffle ticket and a Councilmember for the City of Reedley
ultimately won the prize in a drawing. We advised that "[w]hile it appears from your facts that the
competition was a bona fide competition, the event was held primarily for public officials, and
therefore the raffle was, in part, based on the official status of the attendees."

In the Nemeroff Advice Letter, supra, a nonprofit corporation held an annual golf
tournament for community leaders, government relations professionals, and public officials that was
widely publicized and open to all. It was estimated that public officials would likely to make up
about 20 percent of the 150 tournament participants. Raffle tickets were to be distributed to
participants so the expectation was that public officials would make up a small percentage of
the ticket holders. We therefore advised that because raffle tickets would be distributed "not only to
public officials, but to all persons attending the event, including persons not subject to the Act, the
competition would not be related to the recipient's status as an official." We stressed, however, that
our advice would be different if the ticket holders were primarily officials or candidates regulated
by the Act. We then stated:

Our advice is contingent on your assumption that tickets will not be
limited to public officials. We have not prescribed a fixed percentage of
ticket holders who may not be public officials, but to qualify for the
exception of Regulation 18946.5 [now 18942(a)(14)], members of the
public included in the raffle pool must be sufficiently numerous that a
reasonable person would not infer that such persons were included only
to evade the obvious intent of the rule.

In the present situation, you state that the 2015 CSDA Annual Conference is representative
of the expectations for the upcoming CSDA conference. At the 2015 conference, a majority of the
768 attendees were employees or board members of special districts or other local governments
subject to the Act. Approximately 133 of the attendees were exhibitors, most of whom were not

4 If a prize is considered income, the amount the official may accept is not constrained by the gift limits to the
Act (Smith Advice Letter, No. A-94-356.)

File No. A-16-115
Page No. 6

public officials subject to the Act. Thus, in contrast to the situation in Nemeroll, it is likely that vast
majority of ticket holders will be public officials. We do not view 13.3 out of 768 attendees as being
"sufficiently numerous" to trigger the exception under Regulation 1 8942(a)(14). Therefore, similar
to our advice in Pavlovitch, we find that the upcoming raffles at the 2016 CSDA Annual Conference
will be held primarily for public officials, and the raffles will be, in part, based on the official status
of the attendees.

Accordingly, if any of the raffles are won by public officials with reporting obligations
under the Act, it will constitute the making of a gift to them. Nevertheless, a gift from a donor is
neither accepted nor received if it is returned, donated, or reimbursed in any manner set forth
below:

(1) The gift is returned to the donor, or to the donor's agent or intermediary, unused, within
30 days of receipt or acceptance. In such event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required
to disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity expense; or

(2) The gift is donated, unused, to a charitable organization within 30 days of receipt or
acceptance, without being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes. In such event,
neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to disclose the receipt or making of a gift or
activity expense; or

(3) The gift is donated, unused, to a state, local, or federal government agency, within 30
days of receipt or acceptance, without being claimed as a deduction for tax purposes. In such event,
neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to disclose the receipt or making of a gift or
activity expense; or

(4) The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, reimburses the donor, or the
donor's agent or intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift. In such event, the value of the gift is
reduced by the amount of the reimbursement, and the amount of any gift or activity expense which
must be disclosed is reduced by the amount of the reimbursement. (Regulation 1 8941(c).)

Therefore, any of the actions detailed in Regulation 1 8941(c) may be taken with respect to
any raffle prizes won by a public official at the upcoming conference.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

sections.question:
Will raffle prizes won by public officials in three separate random drawings at the CSDA 
Annual Conference attended by public officials and other individuals not regulated by the Act be 
considered gifts to those public officials?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The raffle prizes will be received in a bona fide competition related to the official 
status of the public officials, and the prize donations will constitute a "gift" to those officials within 
the meaning of the Act.

The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as: 
Any payment2 that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the 
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and 
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the 
rebate or discount is made in the reb'\.llar course of business to members 
of the public without regard to official status. 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the receipt 
of gifts by public officials in the following ways: 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is S460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) 
This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals designated in 
Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee designated in his 
or her agency's conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, if the employee 
would be required to disclose the receipt ofincome or gifts from the source of the gift on his or her 
statement of economic interest. (Section 89503.) 
Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any gifts that 
aggregate to S50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on 
the officials' statements of economic interests. Reporting requirements apply to all officials listed in

87201 ), and employees designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code as specified in the code 
(Section 87302(b)). 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift 
or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the 
official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100, 87103(e), 
Regulation 18700.) 
 Section 82044 defines payment. in part, as any "rendering of ... services or anythmg else of value, whether 
tangible or intangible." 
1 A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental 
agency." (Section 82048.)  
However, there are also exceptions to the definition of a gift. One such exception occurs 
when a prize or award is "received in a manner not related to the official's status in a bona fide

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will raffle prizes won by public officials in three separate random drawings at the CSDA 
Annual Conference attended by public officials and other individuals not regulated by the Act be 
considered gifts to those public officials?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The raffle prizes will be received in a bona fide competition related to the official 
status of the public officials, and the prize donations will constitute a "gift" to those officials within 
the meaning of the Act.

CONCLUSION Yes. The raffle prizes will be received in a bona fide competition related to the official status of the public officials, and the prize donations will constitute a "gift" to those officials within the meaning of the Act. FACTS You are seeking advice on behalf of the California Special Districts Association ("CSDA") and would like to know whether raffle prizes awarded to public officials at the CSDA Annual Conference are "gifts" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82028, or whether such prizes qualify for the exception defined in Regulation 18942(a)(14). We take the following facts verbatim from your letter: ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-115 Page No. 2 CSDA is a 501(c)(6) not-for-profit association that was formed to promote good governance and improved core local serves through professional development, advocacy, and other services for all types of independent special districts. CSDA has three types of membership: regular members, associate members, and business affiliates. Regular membership is open only to independent special districts, as defined by Government Code Section 56044, and other public agencies whose governing body is composed of representatives of two or more public agencies except joint powers authorities, dependent special districts, and county and city agencies. Associate membership is open to public agencies which do not meet the eligibility requirements for regular membership. Business affiliates are private sector companies that offer professional services and products to the special district community. All membership classes require the payment of dues. CSDA currently has 1,104 total members. Of those, 833 are regular members, 137 are associate members, and 115 are business affiliates.
